heading: Azure Availability Zones
body:
Azure (Microsoft Azure) maintains multiple data centers within each
region. Azure (Microsoft Azure) groups the data centers into [availability zones](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/availability-zones/az-overview),
which are separate locations within the region. Maintaining data centers in
different physical locations helps Azure (Microsoft Azure) tolerate local failures.

Azure (Microsoft Azure) availability zones aren't available in all
regions. To learn which Azure (Microsoft Azure)
regions maintain availability zones, see the [Azure](microsoft-azure-supported-regions).
In regions where availability zones aren't yet available, Azure (Microsoft Azure) uses
[fault domains](microsoft-azure-fault-domains) to help ensure failure
tolerance.

Atlas uses Azure (Microsoft Azure) availability zones automatically
when you deploy a dedicated cluster to a region that supports them. Atlas splits the cluster's nodes across
availability zones. For
example, a three-node replica set cluster would have one node deployed onto
each zone. A local failure in the Azure (Microsoft Azure) data center hosting
one node doesn't impact the operation of data centers
hosting the other nodes.

> **Note:** Regions with availability zones provide higher
> uptime for dedicated clusters deployed after September 12, 2019.
> Clusters deployed before September 13, 2019 to regions that now offer availability
> zones aren't split across availability zones automatically. To learn more about availability
> zones, see Azure (Microsoft Azure)'s [documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/availability-zones/az-overview).
